genes.gene_name: PAL1; PAL3; PAL4; PAL5; PAL6; PAL8; PAL9

Species.reason: The paper studies maize phenolic metabolism throughout.

Species_Latin_Name.reason: Maize corresponds to Zea mays, consistent with the paper.

Origin_Species.reason: Promoters and TFs are from maize.

Variety.reason: Promoter fragments were cloned from maize B73; protoplasts used for ChIP/transactivation came from B73 × Mo17 F1 seedlings.

Origin_Species_Variety.reason: Methods state promoter DNA was from B73 genomic DNA.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Figure 1 defines PAL as phenylalanine ammonia lyase.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number for PAL is given in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: PAL is named as an enzyme class, but 'PAL family, ammonia-lyase' is not explicitly stated.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: PAL genes are part of the maize phenolic/phenylpropanoid pathway.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Figure 1 places PAL in the general phenylpropanoid part of the pathway.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: PAL is depicted at the start of the general phenylpropanoid pathway, i.e. an entry/upstream step.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper does not describe the PAL reaction in chemical detail.

Primary_Substrate.reason: L-phenylalanine is not explicitly stated in the paper text provided.

Primary_Product.reason: trans-cinnamate is not explicitly stated in the paper text provided.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: PAL is shown in the pathway feeding phenolic compounds including lignins and flavonoids.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The pathway in Figure 1 results in general phenylpropanoids, lignins, and flavonoids.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These PAL genes feed the phenylpropanoid/lignin/flavonoid outputs shown in Figure 1.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The abstract/discussion frame phenolic compounds as important for plant-environment interactions; lignin also implies structural roles.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: PAL promoters were used as baits and are presented as important nodes in the maize phenolic regulatory network.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: The paper states seven PAL promoters were recognized by MYB65, PAL4 was strongly activated by MYB65 in protoplast luciferase assays, and PAL5 was validated as an MBF1.3 ChIP target.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: These methods are exactly the approaches used for promoter-TF analyses in the paper.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: No subcellular localization for PAL proteins is reported.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: PAL is clearly an entry step, but the paper does not test or establish rate-limiting status.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Methods specify etiolated B73 × Mo17 F1 seedling protoplast assays and plant growth conditions.

Promoters.reason: Methods state upstream native maize promoter fragments (~500–2000 bp upstream, often 1 kb) were used as baits; 35S promoter was used for TF constructs in validation assays.

Omics_Data.reason: CAGE data were used to define TSSs; RNA-seq is referenced in integrated network interpretation; no accession is given here.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: MYB65 and MBF1.3 are explicitly linked to PAL promoters; many additional TFs are present in the network.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The discussion mentions rational metabolic engineering of altered lignin or other specialized metabolites, but this specific application claim for PAL nodes is inferential rather than directly stated.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The study indeed focuses on PAL promoters as pathway targets/baits rather than direct functional perturbation of PAL enzymes.

corrections.reason: No EC number for PAL is given in the paper.
